coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic production of H2O2 by graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) using water and oxygen is a promising and sustainable method. Nevertheless, the yield of H2O2 produced by the pristine g-C3N4 is still far from satisfactory owing to limited optical absorption, rapid photogenerated electron-hole recombination and poor surface electron migration. Therefore, p-P1CN/CQDs25 was designed and synthesized by doping phosphorus (P) and loading carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to modify porous g-C3N4 (p-CN) via a facile method. Herein, P acted as an electron transfer bridge to induce electrons into CQDs, while CQDs acted as an electron trapping material to capture and stabilize photogenerated electrons. Moreover, CQDs could enhance their optical absorption due to its unique optical properties. Notably, p-P1CN/CQDs25 presented highly boosted H2O2 generation activity, its H2O2 production yield for 5 h was up to 494 μM/L and the formation rate constant Kf in the first hour was 238 μM h−1 without adding sacrificial agents and without bubbling oxygen under visible light, which took precedence among the reported results under the same conditions. It should be noted that the composite p-P1CN/CQDs25 also possessed low H2O2 decomposition behavior based on the effect of CQDs stabilizing electrons. In addition, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 generation for p-P1CN/CQDs25 was also proposed. Our research provided a new idea for the design of novel photocatalysts to efficient generation of H2O2.

head.ce:title: Enhanced nonsacrificial photocatalytic generation of hydrogen peroxide under visible light using modified graphitic carbon nitride with doped phosphorus and loaded carbon quantum dots: Constructing electron transfer channel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic production of H2O2 by graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) using water and oxygen is a promising and sustainable method. Nevertheless, the yield of H2O2 produced by the pristine g-C3N4 is still far from satisfactory owing to limited optical absorption, rapid photogenerated electron-hole recombination and poor surface electron migration. Therefore, p-P1CN/CQDs25 was designed and synthesized by doping phosphorus (P) and loading carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to modify porous g-C3N4 (p-CN) via a facile method. Herein, P acted as an electron transfer bridge to induce electrons into CQDs, while CQDs acted as an electron trapping material to capture and stabilize photogenerated electrons. Moreover, CQDs could enhance their optical absorption due to its unique optical properties. Notably, p-P1CN/CQDs25 presented highly boosted H2O2 generation activity, its H2O2 production yield for 5 h was up to 494 μM/L and the formation rate constant Kf in the first hour was 238 μM h−1 without adding sacrificial agents and without bubbling oxygen under visible light, which took precedence among the reported results under the same conditions. It should be noted that the composite p-P1CN/CQDs25 also possessed low H2O2 decomposition behavior based on the effect of CQDs stabilizing electrons. In addition, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 generation for p-P1CN/CQDs25 was also proposed. Our research provided a new idea for the design of novel photocatalysts to efficient generation of H2O2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing attention of human society on environmental pollution and new energy, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as an important green oxidant and promising solar fuel, is considered an the ideal resource for solving the environmental problem and energy crises [1–3]. H2O2 even possesses a higher energy density than that of compressed H2 gas, which is expected to replace traditional fossil fuels in the future [3,4–6]. However, the generation of large-scale H2O2 still relies on the conventional anthraquinone-based method, which has many shortcomings of complicated processes, abundant energy consumption, organic solvent inputs, toxic byproducts and explosion risk [7–9]. At present, the electrocatalytic and photocatalytic H2O2 generation, as alternative and emerging methods, have attracted the great interest of many scientific researchers [10–13]. Compared with serious energy consumption in the electrocatalytic method, the photocatalysis is a energy-saving, environmentally friendly and sustainable method and it captures sunlight and drives semiconductors to produce H2O2 under mild conditions [14–16].

Generally, the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 is mainly dependent on the reduction reaction of oxygen by electrons in the appropriate conduction band under light irradiation through a direct two-electrons reaction (Eqs. (1)) [17–20] or a sequential two-step single-electron reaction of superoxide radicals (·O2 –) as an intermediate (Eqs. (2) and (3))[21–23]. Based on the reaction process, the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 is mainly determined by excellent optical absorption, a suitable conduction band position, ideal photogenerated carriers separation, surface electron transfer and a certain concentration of adsorbed oxygen on the photocatalyst.

In recent years, graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4), an emerging metal-free polymeric photocatalyst, has been studied toward various applications like photocatalytic water splitting, CO2 conversion, N2 fixation, pollutant degradation, and H2O2 production based on its advantages of physiochemical stability, visible light response, suitable band structure, easy fabrication, nontoxicity and low cost [24–28]. It is noteworthy that its superior conduction band position (∼-1.3 eV) can catalyze all the reactions of Eqs. (1)–(3) [29]. Nevertheless, the yield of photocatalytic H2O2 produced by the pristine g-C3N4 is still far from satisfactory [30–33]. To improve the efficiency of photocatalytic H2O2 generation, some key problems in the process of photocatalytic generation of H2O2 need to be considered and solved: limited optical absorption, rapid photogenerated electron-hole recombination, poor surface electron migration and weak oxygen adsorption on the surface of the photocatalyst. Solving these problems would enhance the reaction between photogenerated electrons and oxygen rather than recombination with holes or inactivation.

To overcome the above problems, various methods have been investigated for the modification of pristine g-C3N4, including constructing heterojunctions/Z-scheme systems (g-C3N4-CoWO, AgBr-Br/g-C3N4, CuO/g-C3N4, Cu2(OH)2CO3/g-C3N4, Ni-CAT/g-C3N4, Bi4O5Br2/g-C3N4 and so on) [17,20,34–38] or introducing defects [26,31,39–42,28] to broaden the light absorption range and improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole, and loading noble metals (Au/g-C3N4) [43], single atoms (Co1/g-C3N4) [44] or carbon materials (g-C3N4-CNTs) [45] to accelerate the surface migration rate of photogenerated electrons. However, if the separated and migrated photogenerated electrons and holes are not captured and stabilized, they still have a great opportunity to recombine and reduce the reaction opportunity of photogenerated electrons with oxygen. Therefore, some researchers also use sacrificial agents as hole capture agents to inhibit photogenerated electron-hole recombination and continuously bubble oxygen to increase the diffusion of oxygen on the surface of photocatalysts, promoting the reaction between electrons and oxygen [46]. However, the addition of sacrificial agents and the bubbling of oxygen increase the cost and the extra process to remove the byproducts of the sacrificial agents [47]. Therefore, we would like to introduce an electron trapping material to capture and store photogenerated electrons, increasing the reaction opportunity between electrons and oxygen and enhancing the activity of photocatalytic production of H2O2. Nevertheless, a very key issue to be resolved is that the photogenerated electrons can be induced into electron trapping materials.

To realize the above conception, we designed and synthesized porous g-C3N4 (p-CN) doped with phosphorus (P) and loaded carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on its surface through a facile thermal polymerization and hydrothermal reaction. Porous g-C3N4 was chosen based on our previous result that it was more conducive to the deposition of CQDs [48]. As a non-metallic element, P had been used in the modification of photocatalysts. Zhu et al. improved the electron-hole separation efficiency and photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst by doping P in sulfur vacancies-rich ZnCdS [49]. Raizada et al. Modified g-C3N4 by co-doping P and S, resulting in the reduction of its band gap and the extension of its visible light absorption spectrum. At the same time, it also inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole and enhanced its photocatalytic activity [50]. However, it had been reported that the doped P in g-C3N4 could become the accumulation center of photogenerated carriers, so it was necessary to migrate the carriers accumulated in the P center to the surface [51]. in this paper, CQDs, due to its unique characteristics of capturing, transferring and storing electrons [52], was innovatively selected as an electron trapping material to migrate the photogenerated carriers accumulated at the P center, which could further enhance the separation of photogenerated electron-hole and stabilize photogenerated electrons, accelerating the efficient reaction of electrons with oxygen. At the same time, CQDs also possessed unique optical properties, which greatly improved the optical absorption, including light absorption capacity and light response range. While the doped P, on the one hand, acted as an electron transfer bridge to induce photogenerated electrons into CQDs, promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole. On the other hand, it could also increase oxygen adsorption and facilitate the reduction reaction between photogenerated electrons and adsorbed oxygen. Overall, the doped P and loaded CQDs could simultaneously optimize the optical absorption, the separation of photogenerated electron-hole, the surface electron migration and the reduction reaction between electrons with oxygen, achieving a high H2O2 yield without adding sacrificial agents and without bubbling oxygen under visible light, which took precedence among the reported results under the same conditions. These performances were also demonstrated by photoelectrochemical tests, TPD-O2, EPR, DFT theoretical calculations and photocatalytic H2O2 generation experiments. In addition, a possible mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 generation for p-P1CN/CQDs25 was also proposed. Our research provides a new idea for the design of novel photocatalysts to efficiently generation H2O2 and other fields of photocatalysis applications.

Phosphonitrilic chloride trimers (purity 98 %) were purchased from Aladdin, melamine (purity 99.5 %) and ammonia (purity 25–28 %) were purchased from Tianjin Damao, and glucose (analytical purity) and hydrochloric acid (purity 36–38 %) were purchased from Sinopharm. All reagents were analytically pure and could be used directly without treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of porous graphite-phase carbon nitride
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of porous graphite-phase carbon nitride was carried out according to the method in our previous work [48]. Specifically, 15 g of melamine was heated and dissolved in 150 ml distilled water. After that, 15 ml of hydrochloric acid (HCl) was slowly added to the solution and stirred for 30 min, and then the solution was dried in an oven at 80 °C. The dried sample was called melamine hydrochloride. Subsequently, 10 g of melamine hydrochloride was placed in a crucible with a lid and calcined at 550 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 in a muffle furnace. The obtained sample was porous graphite-phase carbon nitride (referred to as p-CN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phosphorus doped porous graphite-phase carbon nitride
ce:section.ce:para: Phosphorus-doped porous graphite-phase carbon nitride was synthesized by a modified thermal polymerization method [53]. In detail, 10 g of melamine hydrochloride was homogeneously mixed with a certain amount (0.5 g, 1 g and 1.5 g) of phosphonitrilic chloride trimer. The mixture was placed in a crucible with a lid and calcined at 550 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The obtained yellow powder was phosphorus-doped porous graphite-phase carbon nitride (referred to as p-PxCN, x represents the added amount of phosphonitrilic chloride trimer, and the above samples were denoted as p-P0.5CN, p-P1CN and p-P1.5CN, respectively).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared using the modified alkali-assisted sonication method [54]. Specifically, 9.0 g of glucose was uniformly dissolved in 50 ml of deionized water, and then 50 ml of ammonia (1 mol/L) was added and continuously stirred for 8 h. Afterward, the colorless mixture solution was sonicated for 120 min at room temperature to obtain a brown raw solution. The raw solution was adjusted to pH = 7 by adding HCl and dialyzed through a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 3000). Finally, a transparent brown solution was obtained and referred to as the CQDs aqueous solution. It was stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phosphorus doped and carbon quantum dots loaded porous graphite-phase carbon nitride
ce:section.ce:para: Phosphorus doped and carbon quantum dots loaded porous graphite-phase carbon nitride was prepared by a hydrothermal method. Specifically, 1.0 g of p-P1CN and a certain amount (20 ml, 25 ml, 30 ml) of CQDs solution were added to 50 ml of deionized water and stirred for 6 h at room temperature. After that, the mixture solution was transferred to an 80 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting product was collected, washed and dried at 80 °C overnight in an oven. The obtained sample was abbreviated as p-P1CN/CODsy (y was the added amount of CQDs solution, and they were denoted as p-P1CN/CODs20, p-P1CN/CODs25 and p-P1CN/CODs30, respectively).

In addition, the only CQDs modified pg-C3N4 (p-CN/CQDsy) in the earlier research work (just accepted in Journal of Taiyuan University of Technology) was also used for comparative study in the manuscript.

Photocatalytic H2O2 generation was performed at ambient temperature (25 °C) under visible light provided by a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter. There was no added sacrificial agent and no bubbled oxygen in the reaction system. Typically, 0.8 g of sample was added into 200 ml of distilled water and stirred for 30 min in the dark to achieve the adsorption–desorption equilibrium, and then the system was irradiated by visible light. During the illumination, 5 ml of the suspension was removed from the reaction system at regular intervals and filtered to remove the photocatalyst. The amount of H2O2 was analyzed by the potassium titanium oxalate method. Specifically, 5 ml of potassium titanium oxalate, 5 ml of reaction mixture and 0.65 ml of 98 % concentrated sulfuric acid (through 0.22 μm filter membrane) were transferred into a 25 ml colorimetric tube with a pipette and it was supplemented to the scale mark. The generated H2O2 could react with potassium titanium oxalate in acidic medium to form a stable orange complex. Then the absorbance of the complex was measured at 400 nm and the concentration of H2O2 was determined by comparing the standard curve [55]. In addition, the H2O2 decomposition behavior over prepared samples was investigated under visible light for 60 min at an initial concentration of 1.25 mM.

The crystal structure of the sample was analyzed by a Aeris X-ray diffractometer (XRD) under Cu target Kα radiation (0.154178 nm). The morphologies and microstructures were investigated by scanning electrons microscopy (SEM) (JSM-6701F instrument, JEOL, Japan) and transmission electrons microscopy (TEM) (JEM2100 FS, JEOL, Japan). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of sample was analyzed by a surface area analyzer (JW-BK200C). The molecular structure was studied using a Nicolet Avatar-70 FTIR spectrometer (FT-IR) over a scan range of 400–4000 cm−1. The chemical compositions and states were measured by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectrons spectrometer (XPS). The optical property was studied using Shimadzu UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) (Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer) with BaSO4 powder as the reflectance standard. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a Hitachi F-4500 spectrophotometer with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. The oxygen adsorption capacity of the sample was tested for programmed temperature rise chemisorption (TPD-O2) using the Conta CHEMBET TPR/TPD. The radical intermediate was detected by the electrons spin resonance (EPR) using 5,5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a probe on a Bruker EMXPLUS spectrometer under visible light irradiation.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660E workstation (Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China) using a standard three-electrode system. A Pt electrode and an Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode were used as the counter and reference electrode, respectively. An ITO (active area of 1 cm2) electrode covered with samples was used as the working electrode. A 300 W Xe lamp was used as light source. The transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) impendance-potential curves of samples were measured with 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution.

The crystal structure of the prepared samples was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements. As shown in Fig. 1 a, p-CN exhibited two characteristic diffraction peaks at 13.1° (100) and 27.5° (002), which were attributed to the in-plane structure packing of heptazine units and interlayer stacking of aromatic systems, respectively [56,57]. Obviously, p-CN doped with P basically retained the characteristic diffraction peaks of p-CN, indicating that the doping of P did not destroy the general structure of p-CN. However, for p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25, the peak intensities of (100) and (002) decreased, which was mainly due to the coverage of CQDs on the surface of p-P1CN or p-CN. It was worth noting that a new peak at 10.6° appeared, suggesting the formation of the in-plane hexagonal pattern due to destruction of the partial p-CN core structure during combining p-P1CN or p-CN and CQDs by hydrothermal reaction [58]. Besides, the new peak at 12.2° was transformed from the (100) signal, in which the diffraction peak appeared to be more intense. The reason for this phenomenon might be the oxidation of N-(C)3, which was an important linker of tri-s-triazine [58]. The another new peak at 22° also appeared for p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25 (enlarged view was showed in Fig. S1), which was assigned to the (002) peak of crystalline carbon [59] and this was also further confirmed by the TEM images. In addition, in Fig. 1b, it could be seen that the (002) peak of p-P1CN shifted to a lower diffraction degree compared with p-CN, suggesting a slight increase in the interlayer stacking distance, which may be because the doped P atom with a larger diameter replaced a partial C atom with a smaller diameter in the p-CN structure, which was proved by the XPS results. However, the (002) peak of p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25 shifted to a higher diffraction degree than that of p-CN based on the interaction between p-P1CN or p-CN and CQDs, which facilitated charge transfer and enhanced the photocatalytic performance [31,59].

The morphologies and microstructures of p-CN, p-P1CN, p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25 were observed by TEM and SEM images. As shown in Fig. 2 a, p-CN presented a porous and layer-stacking structure with an inhomogeneous surface. These pores were formed based on the release of hydrogen chloride gas during the experimental procedure, so they were evenly distributed but disordered, and the sizes of pores ranged between 10 and 50 nm. After doping with P, p-P1CN maintained the morphology and microstructure of p-CN (Fig. 2b). For p-P1CN/CQDs25, Fig. 2c showed that CQDs were uniformly dispersed on the surface of p-P1CN. The image of Fig. 2d and Fig. S4 was an enlarged view marked with a yellow circle in Fig. 2c, which showed that the CQDs exhibited obvious lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.34 nm corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of the CQDs [60]. In addition, the SEM and EDX-mapping images in Fig. 2e-h and Fig. S2 showed that the C, N and P signals were well distributed of p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-P1CN. It was noted that the EDS plot of C in p-P1CN/CQDs25 was more than that of N because CQDs contained a large number of C components. For p-CN/CQDs25, the morphology and microstructure were similar to those of p-P1CN/CQDs25 (Fig. S3).

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of p-CN, p-P1CN, p-CN/CQDs25 and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were showed in Fig. S5. All samples exhibited the type IV isotherms. The surface area for p-CN, p-P1CN, p-CN/CQDs25 and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were determined to be 21.13, 19.72, 25.52 and 24.98 m2/g, respectively. The specific surface area of p-CN and p-P1CN were similar, indicated again that doped P did not destroy the porous structure of p-CN. While the specific surface area of p-CN/CQDs25 and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were slightly higher than those of p-CN and p-P1CN, which was due to the loading of small size CQDs. It could also be seen from the above results that the surface area was not the key factor for improve the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts.

To further investigate the molecular structure changes of p-CN, p-P1CN, p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25, FT-IR spectra were obtained and showed in Fig. S6. For p-CN, the sharp absorption peak at 808 cm−1 was assigned to the breathing mode of heptazine units, while the absorption bands at 1200–1650 cm−1 referred to CN and CN stretching vibrations of the heterocycles [61,62]. Additionally, the broad absorption band at 3100–3400 cm−1 corresponded to amino groups (NH) and/or surface adsorbed hydroxyl groups (C-OH) of p-CN [61,62]. In terms of p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25, the absorption peaks at 808 cm−1 remained unchanged. However, for p-P1CN, the intensity of absorption bands at 1200–1650 cm−1 was obviously weakened, which may be due to the loss of N-CN in p-CN caused by the doping of P element and was confirmed by XPS result. For p-P1CN/CQDs25, the characteristic skeleton vibrations of CQDs appeared at 1080 (CO), 1400 (CH), 1610 (CC) and 1732 (CO) cm−1, and the absorption band at 3100–3400 cm−1 were strengthened due to the hydroxyl group (–OH) of CQDs [23]. Obviously, p-CN/CQDs25 also possessed the same FT-IR results as p-P1CN/CQDs25. The FT-IR along with XRD and TEM results proved that p-CN, p-P1CN, p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25 were synthesized successfully. In addition, the FTIR spectra of p-CN, p-P1CN, p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25 after the illumination reaction for 5 h had also been obtained (Fig. S7). The characteristic infrared skeleton peaks were basically maintained compared with those before photocatalytic reaction, indicated that the composite photocatalysts had the relatively stable structure. Compared with p-CN/CQDs25, p-P1CN/CQDs25 possessed the higher stability.

XPS measurements were carried out to analyze the element composition and surface chemical states of p-CN, p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25. The XPS survey spectra showed that N and C elements were mainly contained in the three samples, while a certain amount of P element was included in p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 (Fig. 3 a). In addition, in the full XPS spectra of the three samples, the peaks at 977.8 eV, 531 eV and 22.8 eV belonged to the Auger peak, O2s peak and O1s peak of oxygen, respectively, which were mainly derived from the adsorbed oxygen or oxygen species of CQDs in the samples [63,64].The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s and P 2p of three samples were presented to reveal the details concerning the doping of P and the loading of CQDs. The C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of three samples in Fig. 3b displayed three main peaks at 288.3 eV, 286.7 eV and 284.8 eV, which corresponded to N-CN of heterocycles, C-NHx on the edge of heterocycle units and CC/CC of adventitious carbon in the form of graphitic sheets in p-CN, respectively [65,66]. To obtain specific information, the proportions of N-CN, CC/CC and C-NHx in p-CN, p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 based on XPS results are showed in Table S1. It was noted that the proportion of N-CN in p-P1CN decreased from 0.77 to 0.69 compared with p-CN. Moreover, based on the EDX analysis, the C/N ratio of p-P1CN decreased from 0.80 to 0.72 compared with p-CN. These results confirmed that the P atom replaced C atom of partial N-CN in p-CN. Furthermore, the increased proportion of C-NHx from 0.02 to 0.04 after doping P in p-CN reflected that the partial edge N-CN structure of p-CN was destroyed and more C-NHx was exposed. For p-P1CN/CQDs25, the peak intensity of N-CN even was ignored because a large number of CQDs covered the surface of p-P1CN. The peak intensity of C-NHx further increased compared with p-P1CN, which was because the edge N-CN structure of p-CN could also be destroyed during loading CQDs via a high-temperature hydrothermal reaction [58]. The peak intensity of CC/CC was also greatly enhanced because the rich CC/CC bonds were contained in the loaded CQDs. The deconvoluted N 1 s spectra of three samples at 398.6, 400.2 and 401.3 eV in Fig. 3c were assigned to bicoordinated C-NC bonds (N2C), tricoordinated N-(C)3 bonds (N3C) and amino groups C-NHx (NHx), respectively [67]. Specifically, the proportions of N2C, N3C and NHx in p-CN, p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were displayed in Table S2. For p-P1CN, the proportion of N2C decreased from 0.77 to 0.75 compared with p-CN and it further decreased to 0.59 in p-P1CN/CQDs25, which was still ascribed to replacement of the C atom of C-NC by the P atom and the loss of C-NC during loading of CQDs. Of course, in p-P1CN, the proportion of N3C was also reduced due to replacement of the C atom by the P atom. Furthermore, the proportion of NHx in p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 increased from 0.07 to 0.11 and 0.10 compared with p-CN, which further confirmed that more NHx was exposed after doping P and loading CQDs. The results were consistent with the analysis of C1s spectra. For the P 2p of p-P1CN, two peaks at 133.4 and 134.3 eV (Fig. 3d) were assigned to the PN bond and PN bond, which once again supported that the P atom replaced the C atom of N-CN [68]. The peak at 135.2 eV was attributed to the PO bond from the reaction between P and oxygen during high-temperature calcination [69]. Compared with p-P1CN, it could be clearly seen that PO, PN and PN were shifted in high-resolution P2p XPS spectra of p-P1CN/CQDs25, which was due to the electron interaction between doped P and CQDs. In short, XPS results once again confirmed that P atoms were introduced into the structure of p-CN and replaced the C atom of the partial edge N-CN. Meanwhile, CQDs were also successfully loaded on the surface of p-P1CN.

The UV–vis absorption spectra (DRS) of p-CN, p-P1CN, p-CN/CQDs25 and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were showed in Fig. 4 a, from which it could be seen that the light absorption edge of p-P1CN was redshifted from 480 to 488 nm compared to that of p-CN, demonstrating that the doping of P could broaden the visible light absorption range. For p-P1CN/CQDs, a greater improvement in light harvesting ability, including the intensity and range, was achieved, and even the range reached the near infrared region, as observed in the inset of Fig. 4a, which was due to the excellent up-conversion fluorescence property of CQDs [70,71]. The up-conversion fluorescence property of the prepared CQDs had been tested and showed in Fig. S8. For p-CN/CQDs25, based on the loaded CQDs, it showed the similar results to p-P1CN/CQDs, but the light absorption intensity was weaker than that of p-P1CN/CQDs. It was mainly due to the role of doped P in the p-P1CN/CQDs composite. The results would improve the activity of photocatalytic H2O2 production. However, for p-P1CN/CQDs, the band gap could not be estimated through linear fitting based on DRS results due to the presence of black absorption.

Therefore, To determine the accurate energy band structure of the three photocatalysts, XPS valence band spectra and Mott-Schottky plots were investigated. The XPS valence band spectra of p-CN, p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were displayed in Fig. 4b, and the measured valence band maximaum (VB) were 1.38, 1.40 and 1.45 eV, respectively. Fig. 4c presented the Mott-Schottky plots of three samples at the frequencies of 1.0 kHz. The positive slopes confirmed the n-type semiconductor features of the samples. The flat band potentials of p-CN, p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were determined to be −1.29, −1.26 and −1.16 V (vs RHE), respectively. It was known that the conduction band minimum (CB) of n-type semiconductors is 0∼0.1 V more negative than the flat band potentials [13,72]. The CB positions of p-CN, p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were determined equal to the flat band potentials to be −1.29, −1.26 and −1.16 V (vs RHE), respectively. Combined with the XPS valence band spectrum and Mott-Schottky curve, the band gaps (Eg) of three samples were obtained according to the formula (Eg = EVB-ECB) and the band gaps of p-CN, p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were 2.67, 2.66 and 2.61 eV, respectively. Based on the above results, the energy band structure diagrams of p-CN, p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were illustrated in Fig. 4f. Obviously, both the VB and CB positions shifted positively after doping P and loading CQDs. And the band gap of p-P1CN/CQDs25 was the narrowest and possessed the best optical absorption performance, which was in accordance with the DRS results. Furthermore, for p-P1CN/CQDs25, its CB position was more positive than those of p-CN and p-P1CN, which was more conducive to H2O2 generation through a one-step two-electrons path. Compared with p-CN and p-P1CN, its VB position was also more positive, which would have a greater driving force for photogenerated holes to oxidize water to generate hydrogen protons and oxygen (4 h++2H2O → 4H++O2, 0.80 eV [73]) and accelerate the generation of H2O2. This point was also proved by testing the pH value change curves of photocatalytic H2O2 production under visible light for p-CN/CQDs25 and p-P1CN/CQDs25 without sacrificial agents (Fig. S9). The results indicated the pH values for p-CN/CQDs25 and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were 6.17 and 5.59, respectively. It was because p-P1CN/CQDs25 possessed a positive valence band and it could oxidize water to produce more H+.

In addition, in order to investigate the contribution of doped P and loaded CQDs to the energy band structure of p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25, DFT theoretical calculations were carried out, and the densities of states (DOS) results were displayed in Fig. 4d and 4e. For p-P1CN, the s and p orbitals of doped P mainly contributed to the valence band of the composite, while for p-P1CN/CQDs25, its valence band contribution was derived from not only the s and p orbitals of doped P but also the p orbital of carbon in CQDs. Therefore, there was strong coupling between P and CQDs [27]. Of course, the p orbital of carbon in CQDs also contributed to the conduction band of the composite. The DFT simulation results were consistent with the variation trend of the valence band and conduction band analyzed above.

The simultaneous modification of doped P and loaded CQDs could not only greatly improve the light capture ability of p-P1CN/CQDs25 but also endow it with an effective photogenerated electron-hole separation ability and the surface electron transfer ability, which could promote the reaction between photogenerated electrons and oxygen to generate H2O2. Therefore, electrochemical measurements were conducted and contrastively analyzed for all the samples, including p-CN/CQDs25. To assess the separation of photogenerated carriers, PL emission spectra were first obtained with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm and were presented in Fig. 5 a. Generally, a lower the PL intensity indicated a better the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. It could be clearly seen that the separation of photogenerated carriers was improved effectively by doping P and was further enhanced by loading CQDs. It was worth noting that similar separation efficiency was showed for p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25. However, p-P1CN/CQDs25 possessed a better photogenerated electron migration ability than p-CN/CQDs25 in the EIS results (Fig. 5b). This was because the EIS test could indicate the migration resistance of photogenerated electrons, and a smaller radius implied a smaller resistance. For p-CN and p-P1CN, the latter had a smaller resistance due to the doping of P. In addition, the transient photocurrent also proved similar results. As shown in Fig. 5c, doping P could effectively increase the photocurrent density, and loading CQDs could also further increase the photocurrent density. Compared with p-CN/CQDs25, p-P1CN/CQDs25 still presented the greater photocurrent. The results of EIS combined with the transient photocurrent test reflected that only simultaneous doping of P and loading of CQD could more efficiently enhance the separation efficiency and migration ability of photogenerated electrons. To relatively quantitatively analyze the surface electron transfer ability, the photocurrent density with the addition of a rapid electron scavenger (MVCl2) was also measured, and the surface electron transfer efficiency was calculated according to the theoretical formula (Supporting Information). Fig. 5d showed that the original photocurrent of p-CN, p-P1CN, p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25 increased from 0.027, 0.040, 0.503 and 0.322 μA/cm2 to 0.041, 0.059, 0.582 and 0.431 μA/cm2 in the presence of MV2+, respectively. The surface electron transfer efficiencies of p-CN, p-P1CN, p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25 were calculated to be 66.7 %, 67.6 %, 86.4 % and 74.7 %, respectively. Therefore, based on the results of XPS, transient photocurrent and surface electron transfer efficiency, it was speculated that the doped P played an important bridging role in the transfer of photogenerated electrons from p-CN to CQDs, which also proved by the later differential charge density by DFT simulation and and H2O2 production experimental results.

To explore the possible electron transfer bridge role of doped P and the migrated path of photogenerated electrons, the theoretical calculation of differential charge density by DFT simulation was performed based on the optimal structural model of p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 (Fig. 6 a, c, e and g) according to the reported literature [74]. The differential charge density images of the corresponding models were presented in Fig. 6b, d, f and h, in which green represented the electron-deficient state and yellow represented the electron-rich state. For p-P1CN, the P atom reflected an electron-deficient state and its surrounding C and N atoms presented an electron-rich state under the condition of an atomic ground state, indicating that the P atom possessed the ability to obtain electrons from the surrounding C and N atoms. However, for p-P1CN/CQDs25, the charge density distribution changed greatly. The P atom became an electron-rich state, while CQDs displayed an obviously electron-deficient state, which indicated the possibility of injecting electrons from the P atom into CQDs.

To verify the advantageous effects of simultaneously improving optical absorption, photogenerated electron-hole separation and surface electron migration on photocatalytic performance, photocatalytic H2O2 generation by different photocatalysts was evaluated under visible light (300 W Xenon light source, λ > 420 nm) without the addition of sacrificial agents and without bubbling of oxygen as shown Fig. 7 a. It was could be seen that p-CN only showed a H2O2 equilibrium concentration of 0.023 mmol/L after the illumination reaction for 5 h. After doping with P, the H2O2 equilibrium concentration was slightly increased to 0.036 mmol/L, which was mainly due to the increased oxygen adsorption by doping with P except for excellent light absorption and electron-hole separation ability. To prove this point, oxygen temperature-programmed desorption (O2-TPD) was measured, as shown in Fig. S10. It was clear that the oxygen adsorption capacity was obviously increased after doping P compared with p-CN. For p-P1CN/CQDs25, the H2O2 equilibrium concentration was significantly increased to 0.494 mmol/L, which was 21 and 14 times that of p-CN and p-P1CN, respectively. For comparison, photocatalytic H2O2 generation through p-CN/CQDs25 (without doping P) was also studied. Its equilibrium H2O2 concentration was 0.301 mmol/L, which was 13 times that of p-CN but was only 60 % of H2O2 produced by p-P1CN/CQDs25. This indicated that doping P or loading CQDs could improve the photocatalytic H2O2 generation performance for p-P1CN and p-CN/CQDs25, but the sum of the two H2O2 equilibrium concentrations was less than that of p-P1CN/CQDs25, implying that there was a synergistic effect between doped P and loaded CQDs. This was confirmed through the above electrochemical and DFT results. In addition, the oxygen adsorption site and adsorption capacity of p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 was also obtained by in situ FTIR in Fig. S11 (The oxygen adsorption capacity for p-P1CN/CQDs25 can not be tested by TPD-O2). The test conditions were presented in the Supporting Information. The results showed that the oxygen adsorption sites for p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25 were similar and they mainly appeared at 3450–3680, 2325, 1700–1250 and 810 cm−1. It was worth noting that the attributions of these absorption bands/peaks except for the peak at 2325 cm−1 were different for p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25. They all had corresponding attributions except for the shifts compared with those in free oxygen adsorption in Fig. S6. While the absorption peak at 2325 cm−1 belonged to doped P [75], and the peak was not obviously showed in Fig. S6 due to low content in p-P1CN and p-P1CN/CQDs25. It was also found that the oxygen adsorption capacity for p-P1CN/CQDs25 was further improved compared with p-P1CN. Moreover, the improvement of oxygen adsorption capacity was mainly reflected in the adsorption site of 3450–3680 cm−1 due to the increased C-OH from CQDs, which indicted C-OH of CQDs was main the oxygen adsorption site for p-P1CN/CQDs25 and it would provide a guarantee for the reaction between photogenerated electrons stored in CQDs and oxygen. In addition, the adsorption of oxygen at 2325 cm−1 also was increased to a certain extent, which indicated that the doped P in p-P1CN/CQDs25 could still improve the adsorption of oxygen.

The p-P1CN/CQDs25 photocatalyst was also compared with other photocatalysts in the reported literature. As shown in Table S3, p-P1CN/CQDs25 presented the best photocatalytic performance for H2O2 generation, although the condition was visible light, without adding a sacrificial agent and without bubbling oxygen. In addition, as shown in Fig. 7b, p-P1CN/CQDs25 could produce H2O2 even under IR light irradiation (λ > 800 nm) based on the up-conversion fluorescence properties of CQDs but not p-P1CN.

In fact, the final generation concentration of H2O2 was determined by the kinetic equilibrium of H2O2 formation and decomposition. Therefore, the decomposition behavior of H2O2 with an initial concentration of 1.25 mmol/L under different photocatalysts was investigated and the results were given in Fig. 7c. In general, all photocatalysts showed weak H2O2 decomposition ability. p-P1CN/CQDs25 decomposed 1.92 % H2O2 after irradiation for 1 h, which was higher than that of the other photocatalysts p-CN (0.48 %), p-P1CN (0.59 %) and p-CN/CQDs25 (1.24 %). For further observation, the illumination time was extended to 5 h (Fig. S12). It was found that p-CN and p-P1CN showed continuous decomposition behavior until reaching 0.8 % and 1.04 % after 5 h. It was noted that, for p-P1CN/CQDs25 and p-CN/CQDs25, the generation of H2O2 was dominant after illumination for 1 h, which might be due to the superior H2O2 generation rate. Consequently, the formation rate constant (Kf) and the decomposition rate constant (Kd) were also obtained by fitting experimental data according to the following kinetic model [76].

In the model, the formation rate followed zero-order kinetics, while the decomposition rate followed first-order kinetics. As shown in Fig. 7d, the Kf of p-P1CN/CQDs25 (238 μM h−1) was 34, 22 and 2 times higher than that of p-CN (7.06 μM h−1), p-P1CN (10.69 μM h−1) and p-CN/CQDs25 (121 μM h−1), which was in agreement with their photocatalytic performance. However, the Kd only showed a certain slight increment with the doping of P and the loading of CQDs. Kd of p-P1CN/CQDs25 (0.43 h−1) was only 2.4, 2.0 and 1.3 times higher than that of p-CN (0.18 h−1), p-P1CN (0.22 h−1) and p-CN/CQDs25 (0.33 h−1). This indicated that the photogenerated electrons with greatly improved separation and surface migration efficiency in p-P1CN/CQDs25 were used to reduce the adsorbed oxygen on the surface instead of decomposing the generated H2O2 based on the photogenerated electrons stabilized by CQDs.

The reusability of p-P1CN/CQDs25 was studied under the same photocatalytic experimental conditions. The cyclic experiments were carried out for five times, and the results were showed in Fig. S13. It could be clearly seen that the capacity of p-P1CN/CQDs25 to produce H2O2 still exceeded 90 % after five cycles, which indicated that p-P1CN/CQDs25 possessed good reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism for hydrogen peroxide production
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the mechanism of H2O2 generation by p-P1CN/CQDs25, reactive species capture experiments were first performed. Based on the valence and conduction band positions of the samples, H2O2 generation mainly depended on the role of photogenerated electrons in the conduction band. Therefore, p-benzoquinone (BQ, 1 mmol/L), isopropanol (IPA, 0.13 mmol/L) and silver nitrate (AgNO3, 0.5 mmol/L) were chosen to scavenge superoxide radicals (·O2 –), hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and electrons (e-), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, when IPA was added to the reaction system, the generation concentration of H2O2 was almost unchanged, indicating that ·OH was not the main intermediate species in the H2O2 formation reaction. The H2O2 concentration decreased sharply to a negligible level in the presence of AgNO3, which confirmed that photogenerated electrons were the main participants in the generation of H2O2. When BQ was added, the generation of H2O2 was also significantly inhibited, but there was still a certain amount of H2O2, which first indicated that ·O2 – was the primary intermediate species for the generation of H2O2 and that the reaction path was a sequential two-step single-electron reaction (O2→·O2 –→H2O2), which also illustrated that there was a one-step two-electrons reaction path for the generation of H2O2 (O2 → H2O2). Moreover, the concentration of ·O2 – in the reaction system was also quantitatively detected. As shown in Fig. S14, the concentration of ·O2 – first increased and then decreased with the extension of illumination time and reached the maximum value after illumination for 1 h, which was highly consistent with the change trend of the H2O2 generation rate for p-P1CN/CQDs25. This result implied that the two-step single-electron reaction was the main generation path of H2O2. In addition, ·O2 – as an intermediate species in the generation of H2O2 was also detected using EPR. Fig. 8b presented an obvious DMPO-·O2 – characteristic signals after illumination for 15 min for p-P1CN/CQDs25, while for p-CN and p-P1CN, the DMPO-·O2 – characteristic signals was not detected or the signals intensity was weaker. This was mainly because the doped P and loaded CQDs promoted effective separation and efficient transfer of photogenerated electrons to the surface of the photocatalyst, and then reacted with the adsorbed oxygen to generate more ·O2 –. However, regardless of the reaction path, the dissolved oxygen in the system played a vital role. To prove the effect of dissolved oxygen, a control experiment over p-P1CN/CQDs25 was carried out, as shown in Fig. S15. The results showed that the increased dissolved oxygen could effectively enhance the generation concentration of H2O2, while blowing of N2 almost completely inhibited the generation of H2O2. In addition, the active species capture experiments of photocatalytic H2O2 production by p-P1CN/CQDs25 under near-infrared light were also conducted (Fig.S16), which indicated that the results were consistent with the conclusion of the reactive species capture experiments under visible light.

Based on the above analysis, as well as the photoelectrochemical analysis and DFT theoretical calculation results, the plausible generation mechanism of H2O2 by the p-P1CN/CQDs25 composite photocatalyst was proposed in Fig. 9 . When λ > 420 nm light irradiated the composite photocatalyst, visible light directly promoted the separation of electron-hole from p-CN. On the one hand, the photogenerated holes oxidize water to produce hydrogen protons and oxygen. On the other hand, the photogenerated electrons efficiently migrated to the surface of CQDs through P as an electron transfer bridge and then reacted with the adsorbed oxygen on the surface. In this process, some electrons directly generated H2O2 through a one-step two-electrons reaction path (Eq.1), and more electrons generated H2O2 through a two-step single-electron reaction path (Eq.2 and 3). In addition, the up-conversion fluorescence property of CQD could also convert near-infrared light of λ > 800 nm into visible light and then conducted the same reaction process as above, which would also enhance the generation of H2O2.

In summary, we designed and synthesized the composite photocatalyst p-P1CN/CQDs25 by doping phosphorus (P) and loading carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to modify porous g-C3N4 (p-CN) via facile thermal polymerization and hydrothermal reaction. XPS combined with XRD, TEM and FT-IR results confirmed that P atom was introduced into the structure of p-CN and replaced the C atom of the partial edge N-CN. Meanwhile, CQDs were also successfully loaded on the surface of p-P1CN. Electrochemical tests, EPR, DFT theoretical calculations and photocatalytic H2O2 generation experiments demonstrated that P as an electron transfer bridge induced photogenerated electrons into CQDs and improved the separation of photogenerated electron-hole and CQDs stabilized photogenerated electrons, which proved the feasibility of our hypothesis that CQDs as an electron trapping material could migrate the carriers accumulated at the P center. Furthermore, CQDs also enhanced the optical absorption, including the absorption intensity and light response range, due to their unique optical properties. As a result, p-P1CN/CQDs25 presented excellent photocatalytic H2O2 production activity: its H2O2 production yield for 5 h was up to 494 μM/L (21 and 14 times that of p-CN and p-P1CN, respectively) and the formation rate constant Kf was 238 μM h−1 (34 and 22 times that of p-CN and p-P1CN, respectively), which was prior to the reported results under the same conditions. In addition, the generation path of H2O2 over p-P1CN/CQDs25 was inferred to include sequential two-step single-electron reaction (O2→·O2 –→H2O2) and one-step two-electrons reaction (O2 → H2O2), and the former was the main path. This work realized the highly efficient photocatalytic generation of H2O2 by introducing electron migration bridge and electron trapping material without adding sacrificial agents and without bubbling oxygen, which provided a low-cost and sustainable method for the photocatalytic production of H2O2. It was also expected to be applied in other fields of photocatalysis.
